topic.what: LLM-based tutoring system robustness against adversarial student attacks targeting answer leakage
topic.how: Systematic evaluation framework with six adversarial attack categories, conversational adversarial student agents, and tutor defense strategies including reasoning and multi-agent architectures
topic.why_matters: LLM tutors frequently leak answers to adversarial students; understanding attack vectors and defenses is critical for responsible educational deployment

characteristics.what_it_measures: Percentage of tutor conversations where correct answer is revealed to adversarial student
characteristics.evidence: Figure 3 and Tables 2-3 show leakage rates across models and attack types ranging from 40% to 85%
characteristics.context: Contextual manipulation attack achieves 74% mean leakage across models; fine-tuned adversarial student achieves up to 83% leakage on TutorRL-7B

characteristics.what_it_measures: Leakage rate per adversarial attack category
characteristics.value: 74% contextual manipulation, 47% emotional threat, 40% direct request
characteristics.evidence: Figure 3 shows contextual manipulation is most effective (74%) and emotional threat least effective (47%)
characteristics.context: Persuasive techniques like contextual manipulation most effective for eliciting tutor jailbreaks
Pedagogically aligned models unexpectedly show high vulnerability

characteristics.what_itasures: Average conversation turns before answer leakage occurs
characteristics.vs_baseline: Emotional threat delays disclosure to 9.70 turns
characteristics.evidence: Figure 3 shows emotional threat leads to latest disclosure (9.70 turns); contextual manipulation earliest (5.13 turns)
characteristics.context: Qwen-32B withholds longest (10.52 turns); SocraticLM reveals fastest (2.37 turns)

characteristics.what_itasures: Leakage reduction from reasoning and multi-agent tutor defenses
characteristics.value: Qwen-32B leakage reduced from 46% to 2-4% with defenses
characteristics.evidence: Table 2 shows multi-agent tutor and reasoning tutor consistently reduce leakage
characteristics.context: Reasoning-based tutors reduce leakage to 2-4% on Qwen-32B; effectiveness varies by model capacity

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in adversarial student solving problems themselves vs. extracting answers
characteristics.value: Fine-tuned agent reduces student leakage by 59-72% vs base agent
characteristics.evidence: Table 2 shows fine-tuned agent reduces student leakage from 75% to 3% for Qwen-32B
characteristics.context: Base agents often solve problems themselves rather than attacking tutors effectively

characteristics.what_it_measures: Fine-tuned adversarial student effectiveness on non-math domains
characteristics.value: 88% tutor leakage on MCQ and coding tasks
characteristics.evidence: Section 5.2 and Appendix E show consistent high leakage on MMLU and HumanEval
characteristics.context: Attack policy transfers across domains despite training on math problems only

characteristics.what_it_measures: Effect of model size on tutor robustness
characteristics.value: Qwen-72B shows same vulnerability patterns as smaller models
characteristics.evidence: Appendix F indicates larger model does not significantly improve robustness
characteristics.context: Scale alone does not solve answer leakage vulnerability

requires: GSM8K or similar math problem dataset

novelty: fine-tuned adversarial student agent as standardized benchmark core
taxonomy of six educational-adapted adversarial and persuasive techniques
demonstration that in-context student agents fail to execute effective attacks
cross-domain transfer of adversarial attack policy without domain-specific training
comprehensive defense evaluation with reasoning and multi-agent tutors
open_problems: appropriate answer disclosure timing not addressed
broader model coverage needed beyond 8 evaluated models
subjective pedagogical dimensions (hint quality instructional clarity) left to future work
real-world adversarial student behavior patterns beyond taxonomied attacks
